tokens: And when you're up and ready to converse, let me know.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: First thing we've been concerned about - I guess we'll start off with this - is, according to our data, you lost about 25 pounds of water during the post-EVA yesterday; and, it appears that it leaked out during that problem you had with the broken bacteria filter.
What we're wondering is if you've looked around carefully in the cabin, and noticed any sign of that 25 pounds of water.
We suggest looking back behind the ascent engine cover, because it possibly would have run back there (because of the LM tilt) and not have been obvious to you.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: There was some on the floor and the
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: If you find any water back there, we have some suggested procedures to clean it up, and we'd like to do that before depressurization.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Yes, we do have a little puddle of water back behind the engine cover.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Our suggested procedure for collecting that will be to remove the netting and whatever is required to get down to it.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Use a used food bag as a scoop if it's a deep enough puddle to scoop it up and take one of the used (PLSS) LiOH containers that contains a used cartridge that's in the buddy SLSS bag now that's scheduled for the upcoming jettison.
Take the LiOH cartridge out of the container, then use the container itself to hold the water as you scoop it up with the food bag.
And then when you get down to the point where you can no longer get any more water, use utility towels to mop up the rest.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: And if one of you is not busy, I do have a Pad with the lift-off times for rev 32 to 37.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: One more tidbit on the water problem.

According to our figures here, that ETB bag that has the cameras and film you are going to use on the next EVA may be back there where the water is, and we suggest you get it up out so it won't get wet.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: (Long Pause while Jim waits for Fullerton to start reading the Pad) Okay, Gordo.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: If you can remember back to before you went to sleep; this is in reference to the problem you had with the (
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: It was tilted over slightly when we recharged after EVA-1.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Stand by, and I'll see if we want to recharge it or something.
We'll check on what they want to do.
Can you give us a rough idea of what sort of angle it was tilted at and in which direction?
(No answer) (Long Pause) Jim, this is Houston.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Did you copy my last question about...I'll repeat it.
Can you give us an estimate of how far off of vertical the PLSS was in degrees, roughly, when you did fill it?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: It was about 30 degrees off vertical when we charged it.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: That 30 degrees is probably enough to cause the problem you saw at the start of EVA-1; and we may - depending on how you do with cleaning up the water and so forth - want you to top-off the water and maybe the oxygen also on both PLSSs.
We'll have more on that later.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I do have a consumable update for 138 hours, when you are ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: RCS Alfa, 85 percent; Bravo, 85; descent O2 number 1, 71.5; number 2, 68.4; ascent O2 number 1, 99; number 2, 99; descent water, number 1, 42.3; number 2 is 40.2; ascent water, number 1, 100 percent; number 2, 100 percent; and descent amp-hours (remaining) are 1157; ascent amp-hours, 572.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We've got some conversation to come up to you regarding to some updates to the EVA prep and also about the EVA traverse plans.
And if that would fit in with what you're doing, let us know and we'll go ahead and give those words to you.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Well, Dave is off comm right now, so I don't want to talk about the EVA plan or the traverse plan; but maybe EVA prep, I can talk to you about.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: If you get a piece of scratch paper here, I'll give you the camera magazines that we figure should be in the ETB and on the cameras at the start of this.
They've changed slightly from what you loaded last night.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Did Dave tell you what we had loaded last night?
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: I'm not sure; I'll have to check on that.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I don't think we...(Pause) We have the ETB loaded, and let us tell you what we have in ETB.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: The ETB is loaded per checklist (page 5-7) and the additional Mag is Mag Papa.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: That's Mag Papa, is that correct?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: (Pause) Let me cross-check that here and see if we can get any more words on that.
Let me give you one other suggestion and that's for securing the high-gain antenna cable on the Rover (to prevent snags such as occurred at Station 2).

We'd like to suggest that you get the roll of tape out, strip off about 1 foot, and fold a 1- to 2-inch tab on one end of it and wrap that piece of tape around the CDR camera so that after you get down to the Rover you can use it to secure the high-gain cable to the high-gain antenna mast and keep it from flopping around.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Did you copy that about the tape?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: One other thing is we suggest that you each wear your EVA-1 cuff checklist on your right arm for use at the end of EVA-2.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: We've already put that in the ETB.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: And can you give us a post-sleep status report there?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Well, we both slept for the full time, and we'll get the
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: On the ETB-load camera stuff, we'd like you to add Delta Delta - 16-millimeter Mag Delta-Delta - to the bag, and also, if you have your camera loaded with Kilo-Kilo, we'd like you to take it off, put Kilo-Kilo in the bag, and put Papa-Papa on your camera.
Those are the only two changes that we see from what we figure you have right now.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Let me jot down your recommendations again.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Add 16-millimeter Mag Delta-Delta to the bag load, first of all; and then on your 70- millimeter camera, that's the LMP 70-millimeter camera, take off...We assume that Kilo-Kilo is on it now.
We'd like you to take that off and put Papa-Papa onto your camera.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: You're suggesting we take 16-millimeter Delta out(side), and take Mag K off my camera and put Papa on my camera; but probably carry Kilo out with us.
transcript.speaker: Irwin

tokens: Carry Kilo-Kilo with you - and I think you said it right - but take Delta Delta along, also, in the bag.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: First of all, we wondered if you have brought up the LGC (LM Guidance Computer) and cycled it through program 6 there (as per checklist page 5-9).
We haven't got high bit rate and we haven't been able to tell.
The other one was how is the water clean up going and can you estimate how much you're able to scoop up there?
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Yes, the program...Your computer's been cycled.
And all of the water is cleaned up and we got two full LiOH canisters plus about 1/8th of the CSM helmet bag.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: I'll get a suggestion here for what to do with it now; and I'll have it to you in a minute.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: (Pause) It looks like with all the stuff we have to throw over today, we're going to have to use another
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Okay, Dave, you can dump up to one-half of one of those LiOH cans worth into the urine system, if you wish.

And if you can get the rest into the two cans, we suggest you put the lids on, maybe tape them to hold them secure, put them in the Buddy SLSS bag, and then jettison them in the upcoming jettison of that Buddy SLSS bag stuff.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Be sure those tops don't come off until we depress, I guess, huh?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We didn't copy your last transmission.
If it was anything significant, say again.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: I guess we just want to make sure that the tops don't come off the LiOH cans when we get ready to jettison.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And the buddy SLSS bag will just barely hold one cartridge plus the other debris we have.
So I think we're going to have to use a
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: And I guess we have a question on the water in the helmet bag.
Do you think we'll have any problem with that if the bag is hitched up and we keep it in a local vertical position.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: We're just going around the room on that one.
And we'll have an answer in a second here.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, our little helmet bag is beginning to drip, so I guess it probably would be better to transfer that into the urine container, right?
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: In fact we were thinking about suggesting that, so you'll have the helmet bag to stow the visor in, later, for launch.
Be easier than trying to tape it (meaning the visor) down.
Go ahead and stick the urinal right into the bag, and it should suck it right out of there.
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, but we were talking about the helmet bag, not the LEVA bag.
We weren't going to use the LEVA bags.
transcript.speaker: Scott

tokens: Jim, on the subject mentioned earlier about your PLSS being filled on a slant and causing the problem you had with the (
transcript.speaker: Fullerton

tokens: Okay, Jim says he's not worried about it, as long as it's no real problem.
transcript.speaker: Scott
